statement.markdown: > Write a function to square each number in the given array. The function should return a new array containing the squared elements. Note: Avoid using the `map()` function and any other higher-order functions. You must use iteration and loops to implement your solution. The input array will contain a mix of integer and floating point numbers. Be careful not to modify the original array.
statement.inputFormat: An array containing integers and floating point numbers
statement.outputFormat: A new array containing the squared elements
statement.notes: You must use iteration to solve this problem. Don't use map() or higher-order functions.

examples.explanation: The input array [1, 2, 3, 4, 5.5] is squared to produce [1, 4, 9, 16, 30.25].
The input array [10, -5, 0, 0.5] is squared to produce [100, 25, 0, 0.25].

hints.text: First hint text: Try using a for loop to iterate over the array, and a new for loop to calculate the square of each number.

editorial.title: Squaring Every Number in Array Editorial
editorial.intuition: The approach to this problem is to use two for loops to iterate over the array and calculate the square of each number. The squared numbers can then be stored in a new array.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach is to use two nested for loops to iterate over the array and calculate the square of each number. This approach is inefficient and should be avoided.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution to this problem is to use two for loops to iterate over the array and calculate the square of each number. This approach has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness for this problem is to verify that the squared numbers in the output array match the expected squared numbers. This can be done by checking each element in the output array.
editorial.dryRun: Dry run example: Assume we have the array [1, 2, 3, 4, 5.5]. The first for loop will iterate over each element in the array and the second for loop will calculate the square of each number. The squared numbers will then be stored in a new array.

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: vector<double> squareArray(vector<double>& nums); };
starterCode.java: public class Solution { public double[] squareArray(double[] nums); }
starterCode.python: class Solution: def squareArray(self, nums: list[float]) -> list[float]: pass

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: vector<double> squareArray(vector<double>& nums) { int n = nums.size(); vector<double> result; for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { result[i] = nums[i] * nums[i]; } return result; } };
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public double[] squareArray(double[] nums) { int n = nums.length; double[] result = new double[n]; for (int i = n; i >= 0; i--) { result[i] = nums[i] * nums[i]; } return result; } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def squareArray(self, nums: list[float]) -> list[float]: n = len(nums) result = [] for i in range(n): result[i] = nums[i] * nums[i] return result
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function squareArray(nums) { var n = nums.length; var result = []; for (var i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) { result[i] = nums[i] * nums[i]; } return result; }

solutions.python:
class Solution: def squareArray(self, nums: list[float]) -> list[float]: n = len(nums) result = [nums[i]**2 for i in range(n)] return result
print(Solution().squareArray([1, 2, 3, 4, 5.5]))
solutions.cpp: class Solution { public: vector<double> squareArray(vector<double>& nums) { int n = nums.size(); vector<double> result; for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { result.push_back(nums[i] * nums[i]); } return result; } }; int main() { vector<double> nums = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5.5}; Solution solution; vector<double> squaredNums = solution.squareArray(nums); for (int i = 0; i < nums.size(); i++) { cout << squaredNums[i] << ' '; } return 0; }
